text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform an abdominal approach for an end-block right hepatectomy, diaphragm resection, and wedge lung resection targeting a hydrated cyst.
The patient is a thirty-five-year-old female from Ethiopia who was found to have a ten-centimeter stage CE-B, a conicoccus liver cyst that was invading the right hemithorax.
She was consented for surgical resection and started on albendazole the day prior to surgery.
Her liver CT demonstrates involvement of the majority of the right liver, as well as penetration of the right hemithorax with involvement of the diaphragm and possibly the right lung base.
In addition, the cyst is adjacent to the middle hepatic vein, and this complicates the potential resection.
The surgeon uses a fan retractor and makes a steel incision to facilitate the extraction of the hydrated cyst.
Robotic ports are positioned across the upper abdomen, with a 12mm assist port for the Kooza placed between robotic ports one and two.
The robotic setup includes port number two serving as the camera, while the remaining ports function as working and assist arms positioned across the upper abdomen.
The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the falciform ligament, extending the dissection up to the hepatic veins, which are exposed extrahepatically.
The surgeon exposes the middle and left hepatic veins, along with the extrahepatic portion of the right hepatic vein, using robotic assistance.
The surgeon uses intraoperative liver ultrasound to visualize the hydatid cyst and its proximity to the middle hepatic vein.
The surgeon marks the parenchymal transection plane on the liver, aiming to preserve the middle hepatic vein during the hepatectomy.
One of the paradigms of hydatid liver cyst surgery is the use of hypertonic saline in the event of cyst rupture.
Hypertonic saline-soaked gauze is applied over the liver cyst, demonstrating a precautionary measure in case of cyst rupture.
The surgeon initiates inflow control by performing a cholecystectomy, identifying the cystic artery and cystic duct for ligation and removal.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery and duct with Hem-o-lok clips after obtaining the critical view, then divides these structures. The gallbladder is carefully dissected from the cystic plate and removed from the patient.
The surgeon exposes the right liver hilum, using the cystic duct to retract the bile duct medially, thereby revealing the inflow to the right liver.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects and encircles the right hepatic artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon occludes the right hepatic artery with a bulldog clamp prior to its division, ensuring control over blood flow in the area.
The surgeon uses intraoperative liver ultrasound with Doppler to assess the patency of the left hepatic artery, visualizing blood flow within the vessel.
The surgeon clips and divides the right hepatic artery, revealing the underlying right portal vein.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the right portal vein, revealing its structure and surrounding tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects, clips, and divides branches to the caudate lobe and segment six, exposing and gaining control of the right portal vein.
The bifurcation of the right and left portal veins is visualized prior to the division of the right portal vein, showing the anatomical junction between these major vascular structures.
The surgeon divides the right portal vein using a silk tie and secures it with three Hemlock clips before completing the division.

The surgeon completes inflow control for the right hepatectomy by ensuring the right portal vein and right hepatic artery are clipped and divided, securing the vascular structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in fully mobilizing the right hemiliver due to the invasive hydatid cyst and its involvement with the diaphragm, which is visually evident in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the hepatorenal ligament, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon utilizes an interior approach to perform retrohepatic caval dissection, accessing the area behind the liver.
The retrohepatic cava is exposed, and the surgeon circumferentially dissects the short hepatic branches, using hemlock clips for clipping or a robotic vessel sealer for dividing these vessels.
The surgeon controls larger short hepatic vein branches using hemlock clips and divides them, while smaller hepatic vein branches are safely divided with the robotic vessel sealer.
The surgeon places hypertonic saline-soaked gauze in the retroperitoneum, preparing for potential spillage of cystic contents.
Endocyanin green is administered, highlighting the demarcation between the right and left hemi-livers. This visual cue is used to guide the completion of the planned parenchymal transection line.
The surgeon begins the parenchymal transection using a laparoscopic CUSA device, visibly separating liver tissue along the planned transection line.
The surgeon uses the laparoscopic CUSA to dissect intraparenchymal vascular and biliary structures, which are then clipped with hemlock clips or divided using the robotic vessel sealer.
The bedside surgeon operates the laparoscopic CUSA device through the assist port, positioned adjacent to the robotic camera port.
The surgeon performs a precise dissection between the echinococcal cyst and the middle hepatic vein, utilizing the laparoscopic CUSA for careful separation of these structures.
The surgeon divides the right bile duct intraparenchymally, following the demarcation line established by the administration of endocyanin green.
The surgeon encircles the hepatic duct bifurcation and uses an ICG cholangiogram to visually confirm its location, ensuring the confluence and left hepatic duct are not inadvertently injured.
The surgeon applies three hemlock clips to the right bile duct and then divides it, completing the division of the right bile duct.

The surgeon performs parenchymal transection along the lateral border of the retro-hepatic inferior vena cava, progressing until the right hepatic vein is visualized. The vein is then circumferentially dissected and controlled.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to staple the right hepatic vein, completing the parenchymal transection.
After the liver is divided, the surgeon places hypertonic saline-soaked gauze around the surgical field to mitigate the risk of cyst spillage during the diaphragm resection and liver mobilization.
Hypertonic saline-soaked gauze is placed around the liver in the retroperitoneum and along the cut surface of the left liver.
The surgeon opens the diaphragm, revealing the right hemithorax and providing access to the thoracic cavity.
A double lumen endotracheal tube was placed at the beginning of the case so that the right lung can be deflated during this portion.
The surgeon releases the diaphragm, allowing for the complete mobilization of the right liver, which is visibly freed from surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the right triangular ligament, a step that was previously not possible, facilitating further mobilization of the liver.
The surgeon approaches and completes the resection of the posterior aspect of the diaphragm, with the right hemithorax exposed.
The hydatid cyst is visible at the base of the right lung. A small wedge resection of the right lung is performed using a robotic stapler.
The surgeon completes the final resection of the en bloc specimen, visually confirming the removal of the targeted tissue.
The surgeon places the specimen, with the cyst positioned first, into a large endobag and removes it through the fan and steel port.
An ultrasound is performed, showing the patency of the left hepatic artery and left portal vein. The surgeon then begins the reconstruction of the diaphragm.
The thoracic team performs an interrupted zero-ethabon suture reconstruction of the diaphragm, ensuring there is no tension in the repaired area.
The completed diaphragm reconstruction is visible, showing the sutured area without tension.
Pledgets are applied to the diaphragm during reconstruction to prevent tearing, ensuring the integrity of the tissue as sutures are placed.
Patient had an uncomplicated procedure with three hundred fifty cc's of blood loss.
There was no spillage of cystic material during the case and the patient was discharged home on post-operative day number five with normal liver function.
The final specimen is displayed, showing numerous daughter cysts within the hydrated cyst.
Thank you very much for watching and we hope you enjoyed this video.
